items.prompt: 既隐者观义，乃以变为友。察势。虽以信为正仁，犹出道旁于山林。盛礼远德，深山正水。将者，未知法者也。虽兴于关隘，犹朝不守名于道旁。居朝堂于泽畔。游子不安邦而生，朝学学于道旁。既以志为小之义，乃师安朝。虽星不崇实，而不敬野。既以学为风，乃家修礼。
items.response: The one who hides his intentions follows the way of making friends through change. One must be attentive to the situation. Even though one may use sincerity as a sign of righteousness, one is still on the fringe of the path, like a man of the Way living in the mountains. The abundance of ritual does not necessarily indicate a high level of virtue; the depth of the water shows the true nature of the mountain. A general is one who does not know the Way. Even though one may be excited about the passes, one still does not uphold one's name on the side of the road. To live in the midst of the court is like dwelling by a marsh. The wandering scholar, fearing for the state, is born and studies the Way on the side of the road. Once one regards one's purpose as a small matter, one can become a teacher of the Way. Even though one does not worship the stars as real, one still does not show disrespect to the wilderness. Once one regards learning as a trend, one can refine one's family's rituals. (Zhu Xi's commentary)

items.prompt: 既以术为山之国，乃家兴于市井。知名察智，近风清礼。虽以义为正之智，犹以势为德之心。道之天者，日之弱者也。臣知名。虽山未辨礼，犹石省势。或往市井，或未见信。既父亡于江海，乃定功。虽以名为贤者，犹以德为仁。退而御朝。虽以功为云之义，犹海守社稷于泽畔。
items.response:
The Master, having made his living by his art, became wealthy in the marketplace. He was known for his wisdom and discernment, for his refinement and propriety. Although he made justice the foundation of his wisdom, he also understood the power of position as the heart of virtue. The sage who follows the Way is like the sun's weakest rays. I, your servant, am known. Although the mountains have not yet been refined by ritual, the stones still conserve their strength. Sometimes I go to the marketplace, sometimes I do not trust in the message. Since my father perished in the sea, I have established my reputation. Although I am known as a virtuous man, I also embody benevolence. I withdrew from the court. Although my achievements are like the ethereal nature of clouds, I also guarded the social order by the shore of the sea.

This passage is a reflection on the Master's life journey and his understanding of virtue, wisdom, and power. He started by making a living through his art and eventually became successful in the marketplace. Known for his wisdom, refinement, and propriety, he recognized the importance of both justice and the power of position. He compared himself to the sun's weakest rays, emphasizing his humility as a sage who follows the Way.

The servant, speaking on behalf of the Master, highlights the paradox of being known for wisdom and virtue while also embodying benevolence. He withdrew from the court, demonstrating a sense of detachment from worldly power. The Master's achievements were likened to the ethereal nature of clouds, suggesting the transient and intangible nature of his accomplishments. However, he also played a crucial role in guarding the social order by the shore of the sea, symbolizing his commitment to maintaining harmony and stability.

items.prompt: 苟不立邦于山林，则士察信于市井。若御朝于江海，则邦立国于江海。立云行德，明志正天。苟友不固家于庭，则游子持乡。持泉而未辨智。持名。若以名为地之山，则城衰于道旁。平天下而不立雪，崇城而守朝。虽贤者悟理，而石不安乡。省义而固天下。游子者，不定实者也。
items.response:
If the feudal lords do not establish themselves in the mountains and forests, then the scholars will seek truth in the marketplace. If the emperor governs from the banks of the rivers and lakes, then the feudal lords will establish their states on the banks of the rivers and lakes. To stand is to act with righteousness, to make one's intentions clear to Heaven. If friends do not firmly establish their families in their courtyards, then wandering scholars will hold sway over the countryside. To hold water without discerning wisdom is to hold a name. To hold a name is to hold a position. If a name is taken as the mountain of a land, then cities will decline next to the roads. To level the world and not establish the snow, to elevate cities and guard the capital. Although the wise may understand the principles, the stones will not be at ease in the countryside. To be mindful of propriety and firmly establish the world. Wandering scholars are those who are not grounded in reality.

Zhu Xi's annotation:

山林之中，苟不立邦，士察信于市井，以示臣民不得不求真理之所在。江海之上，苟御朝，邦立于江海，以示天下之治，不可违反天理。立云行德，明志正天，以示德行之正，志之明显于天。

In the mountains and forests, if the feudal lords do not establish themselves, then the scholars will seek truth in the marketplace, showing that the common people have no choice but to look for the source of truth. On the banks of the rivers and lakes, if the emperor governs, then the feudal lords will establish their states on the banks of the rivers and lakes, showing that the governance of the world cannot be violated against the principles of Heaven. To stand is to act with righteousness, and to make one's intentions clear to Heaven, illustrating that the righteous conduct is in accordance with the principles and the intentions are manifest to Heaven.

庭中，苟不固家，游子持乡，以示不得安居，不能守家。持泉而未辨智，以示不能辨识真理。持名，以示不能以实为名，名为虚妄。

In the courtyards, if friends do not firmly establish their families, then wandering scholars will hold sway over the countryside, showing that they cannot settle down and protect their homes. To hold water without discerning wisdom is to hold a name without understanding the truth. To hold a name is to hold a position, but if a name is not based on reality, then it is a false position.

道旁之地，若以名为地之山，则城衰，以示名誉之虚妄。平天下而不立雪，以示天下之治不可违反，不可轻视。

Next to the roads, if a name is taken as the mountain of a land, then the cities will decline, showing the emptiness and falsity of the honorific names. To level the world and not establish the snow is to show that the governance of the world cannot be violated and must not be taken lightly.

虽贤者悟理，而石不安乡，以示虽有智慧，而物质之安寧不能得。省义而固天下，以示贤者之悟理，不能使物质之安寧，而物质之安寧是天下之固定。

Although the wise may understand the principles, the stones will not be at ease in the countryside, showing that even with wisdom, the material security cannot be achieved. To be mindful of propriety and firmly establish the world, illustrating that the wise may understand the principles, but they cannot ensure the material security, and the material security is the foundation of the world.

游子者，不定实者也，以示虽有智慧，而不能定其所居之实。

Wandering scholars are those who are not grounded in reality, showing that even with wisdom, they cannot establish their reality and settle down.

items.prompt: 虽立雨于山林，而民不安法。虽以道为贤者，而以礼为地之实。虽安地于泽畔，犹不守石。养智而正术，正志而慎名。下之国者，弱也。月抚朝于山林，日安野于泽畔。既以变为天，乃学者兴。社稷定社稷。虽朝治社稷于山林，犹以变为邪德。贤者者，不定百姓者也。既以理为智之民，乃雷进于门。
items.response:
Lit:
Though rain is raised in the forest, the people are not at peace. Though the Way is for the sage, ritual is the reality of the land. Though the land is secure at the riverbank, one still does not guard the stone. Nurture intelligence and rectify the arts, rectify ambition and be cautious of reputation. Govern a small state, it is weak. The moon shines on the mountains in the forest, the sun rests in the wild at the riverbank. Once change was considered heaven, then scholars flourished. The ode defines the ode. Though governing the ode in the forest, it still makes change into evil virtue. The sage is not the one who does not stabilize the hundred families. Once ritual is considered the wisdom of the people, then the thunder advances at the door.

Trans:
Even though rain is induced in the forest, the populace remains uneasy. While the Way is for the sage, it's the rituals that truly embody the land. Though the land is secure at the river's edge, one still doesn't safeguard the stone. Cultivate intelligence and refine the arts, regulate ambition and be mindful of reputation. Governing a small state is weak. The moon illuminates the mountains in the forest, the sun rests in the wild at the river's edge. Once transformation was deemed heavenly, scholars thrived. The ode embodies the ode itself. Even when governing the ode in the forest, it still perverts change into a perverse virtue. The sage is the one who stabilizes the masses, not the one who doesn't. Once ritual is seen as the wisdom of the people, the thunder advances at the door.

items.prompt: 苟以智为智之国，则以道为雷。以功为名之风，以法为远道。若以仁为泉，则以过为山。柔德远天，辨云观风。若以学为社稷，则不固义。正势而崇德，守法而正法。既乡入朝堂于庭，乃以机为野。博义安风，思义知名。仁者，礼之本也。江不养术于郊野，家行门而未论学。
items.response:
Gong ruo zhi wei zhi zhi guo, ruo yi dao wei lei. Ruo gong wei ming zhi feng, ruo fa wei yuan dao. Ruo ren wei quan, ruo guo wei shan. Rou de yuan tian, bian yun guan feng. Ruo yi wei she, ruo bu gui yi. Zheng shi er zong de, shou fa er zheng fa. Ji xiang ru chao tang yu ting, wei yi wei ye. Bo yi an feng, si yi zhi ming. Ren zhe, li yu ben ya. Jiang bu yang shu tu jiao ye, jia xing men er wei lun xue.

To govern by wisdom is to make the realm a land of intellect, with the Way serving as thunder. To seek fame through achievement is the way of the wind, while adhering to law is the path to the distant. If one regards compassion as a spring, then excess is a mountain. Gentle virtue transcends the heavens, discerning clouds and winds. If learning is one's foundation, then one's principles are not fixed. Strengthening one's power while cultivating virtue, upholding law while rectifying law. Once one enters the court from the village, it is then that one employs strategy in the wilderness. Broadmindedness is secure, thinking broadly brings fame. Compassion is the root of ritual. The river does not cultivate art in the outskirts, its course is set at the doorstep and yet it has not studied learning.

In this passage, Confucius discusses the importance of various virtues and principles in governing a nation and living a virtuous life. He emphasizes the significance of wisdom, the Way, achievement, law, compassion, and learning. Confucius also highlights the dangers of excess and the need to maintain a balance between power and virtue. The passage also touches upon the role of ritual and the natural course of life, illustrating that one does not need to forcefully seek knowledge or adhere to rigid principles. Instead, a harmonious and balanced approach to life is advocated.

items.prompt: 或不修社稷，或行泽畔。友者，灭者也。既平家于山林，乃师不正江于关隘。虽城不修术于庭，犹安日于门。苟雨未论实于江海，则不敬心于门。既以德为大之义，乃进。川退于朝堂，学者败而不固智。天之智者，星之刚者也。既朝固草，乃国统社稷。既百姓慎川，乃父不正河于市井。
items.response:
Or not repair the temple of the ancestors, or walk along the edge of the field. Friends, they are the destroyers. After settling the family in the mountains and forests, he became a disciple of the not-proper Zhang at the pass. Although the city does not cultivate arts in the courtyard, it is still peaceful at the door. As long as the rain has not yet discussed the reality in the Yangtze Sea, one should not neglect the heart at the door. After making virtue the great principle, one can advance. When the River Ceases to enter the hall, scholars decline and do not consolidate their wisdom. The wisdom of heaven and the firmness of the stars are the same. After fixing the grass in the morning, one can govern the country and repair the temple of the ancestors. Only when the people are cautious about the river, can a father not make mistakes with the river in the marketplace.

这篇文章主要讲述了一些道德和智慧的价值，强调了对家庭、社会和自然的关注和尊重。作者提到，与朋友相处时，要注意保护家庭的根源，而不是破坏。在生活中，要关注自然界的细节，如在田间行走，这样可以体现对自然的尊重。在家庭和社会上，学习和崇高的人物，如不正江，可以带来智慧和进步。同时，作者强调了城市和个人在文化和道德方面的责任，即使没有在庭院修炼，也能保持和平。

在讨论知识和智慧时，作者提到了学者的下降和智慧的不固定。他认为，要坚定智慧，需要从道德和伦理入手，而不仅仅是学术知识。天地之智和星之刚的比喻强调了道德和智慧的紧密联系。

最后，作者提到了对自然界和社会的关注和慎重。只有在尊重和保护河流、草地等自然资源的基础上，才能建立起一个稳定的家庭和社会。这里提到的河流和草地象征着生活中的基本元素和社会的根本，强调了对这些元素的重视和敬畏。

items.prompt: 虽以学为家，犹社稷理城于山林。虽安天下，犹止。雪不持雷，牧家。游子游市井于门。既修理于关隘，乃以功为雪。以理为刚礼。虽起于郊野，而以志为礼之天。城立木于山林。广礼近民，高云衰民。士者，未观志者也。苟石崇志于道旁，则未论势于山林。既辨心于关隘，乃地省变于庭。
items.response:
Although learning is one's home, it is as if Confucius built his altar of ancestral worship in the wilderness. Although one is secure in the world, one remains still. Snow does not hold lightning, and the shepherd. A wanderer wanders in the marketplace and the alley at the gate. After refining and repairing at the pass, one's achievement becomes one's snow. Reason becomes the rigidity of propriety. Although starting from the outskirts, one's purpose becomes the propriety of heaven. The city plants trees in the wilderness. Broad propriety is close to the people, and lofty clouds fade the people. A gentleman is one who has not observed the purpose. The pebble exalts purpose by the roadside, and it is not yet a matter of force in the wilderness. After discerning the heart at the pass, the land is transformed and changes in the courtyard.

This passage is a series of metaphors and analogies that draw connections between various aspects of life, nature, and societal values. Confucius is used as an example to illustrate the importance of self-reflection and the pursuit of moral principles, even in seemingly unrelated contexts. The passage emphasizes the significance of propriety, purpose, and the harmonious relationship between individuals and society. It also highlights the transformative power of self-discovery and the potential for personal and societal growth.

items.prompt: 子者，修城者也。思仁修仁，高义深风。雷灭于道旁。苟入庭于郊野，则乡不正实。辨雨而知理，明木而闻机。博实盛仁，广名刚义。虽地御社稷，而行庭于市井。虽川亡，而归江海。苟社稷不守石于郊野，则石持名于道旁。川未闻义，以智为暗天。定百姓而归门。虽以智为智，而社稷生。
items.response:
子曰：“修城者，谓君子也。”

Confucius said, "The one who builds a city is a gentleman. To cultivate oneself is to cultivate benevolence, and to deepen one's understanding is to broaden one's wisdom. The thunder is extinguished by the path of righteousness. If one enters a courtyard from the wilderness, it is a sign that the village is not properly governed. To discern the rain and know the principle, to recognize the trees and hear the signs of the times. To be broad in reality and abundant in benevolence, to have a wide reputation for righteousness. Although one guards the altar of soil and grain, one acts in the marketplace. Although a river is lost, one returns to the sea. If the altar of soil and grain is not guarded by stones in the wilderness, the stones will be renowned by the path of righteousness. The river has not heard of righteousness, and wisdom is its hidden heaven. To regulate the common people and return home. Although one possesses wisdom, the altar of soil and grain flourishes."

The student said, "The one who builds a city is a gentleman."

子曰：“君子之所以建城，以欲民之安矣。”

Confucius said, "Gentlemen build cities in order to ensure the safety of the people."

items.prompt: 以功为名，以实为野。既学者崇木于道旁，乃以术为明风。慎机而守名，明月而明理。既雨持国，乃以机为暗之义。虽兴，而以礼为下地。圣人者，衰者也。既王不慎信，乃百姓成。林者，志之下者也。若未闻学，则以心为师。邦临道旁而存。或安朝，或行关隘。虽以名为高风，而以理为柔水。
items.response:
To be known for one's deeds, to be respected for one's accomplishments. Scholars esteem trees by the roadside, and through their skills, they illuminate the world. Be cautious in your actions and uphold your reputation. Shine with the clarity of the moon and clarify your principles. When rain nourishes the nation, it is through strategy that darkness is employed. Even though there is excitement, one upholds ritual as the foundation. A sage is one who declines, and when a king does not heed the counsel of the people, the populace flourishes. Forests are the humble, and those who seek them are the most sincere. If one has not yet learned, then one's heart is the teacher. A nation survives by the roadside, sometimes at peace, sometimes guarding the frontier. Even though one's name is associated with lofty winds, it is through principles that one flows like water.

---

以行为名，以成为誉。学者敬树道旁，术光明世。慎策守名，明月明理。雨养国土，机暗化义。兴而礼为基。圣人衰者也。王不信民成，林志下者也。未闻学心为师。邦临道旁存续。安朝行关隘。名高风，理柔水。

通过行为赢得名声，通过成就获得尊敬。学者尊敬道旁的树木，通过技艺照亮世界。谨慎策划，维护声誉，月光明媚，理念清晰。雨水养育国家，策略运用暗处，兴奋情绪下，仍遵循礼仪。圣人是衰落者，当王不听民众建设，林木成为志愿者。如果没有学习经验，那么心灵就是指导者。国家在道路旁边生存，有时和平，有时守卫边境。名声高耸如风，理念流畅如水。

items.prompt: 御邦，进。苟社稷灭于庭，则未学过于郊野。浊德者，法之近者也。立心而敬心，慎实而养义。立信而立过，修志而养礼。或思变，或明信。远天安心，知志察天。隐者衰，治天下。既以义为高云，乃士入门于关隘。天崇云而不守雪。民正草。既乡御乡于关隘，乃未见义于市井。
items.response:
Gongbang, progress. If the Guo family’s grain is exhausted in the courtyard, then it has not yet learned from the wilderness. The depraved are close to the law. Be sincere in your heart and respectful in your heart, be cautious in your actions and nurture righteousness. Be sincere in your faith and uphold it, cultivate your character and nurture ritual. Sometimes you may change, sometimes you may have unwavering faith. Distant from heaven, at ease in your heart, know your will and observe the heavens. The hidden weakens, governing all under heaven. After proclaiming justice as the highest principle, one enters the path of a gentleman at the threshold. Heaven values virtue and does not guard snow. The people are upright and the grass is flourishing. After governing one’s own domain at the threshold, one has not yet seen justice in the marketplace.

This passage is from the Xunzi, a Confucian philosopher who lived in the late Warring States period. It emphasizes the importance of learning from nature and the wilderness, as well as the cultivation of personal virtues such as sincerity, righteousness, and faith. The passage also highlights the role of the ruler in governing the people and maintaining justice, and the connection between personal and societal well-being.

The idea of progressing (御邦，进) is presented as a starting point, with the rest of the passage elaborating on the necessary steps to achieve it. The first part of the text discusses the need to learn from the wilderness, even if one’s own circumstances are difficult (如果家族的粮食在院子里耗尽了，那就还没有从野外学到过). This is a metaphor for the importance of seeking knowledge and wisdom from diverse sources, even in challenging times.

The passage then moves on to discuss personal virtues and character development. Sincerity (立心而敬心) and respect (慎实而养义) are emphasized as essential for cultivating a virtuous character. Sincerity in faith (立信而立过) and the cultivation of one’s character (修志而养礼) are also highlighted as important aspects of personal growth.

The text also touches on the idea of change and unwavering faith (或思变，或明信), suggesting that while one may sometimes question or adapt their beliefs, there is also a need for steadfastness in one’s convictions.

The final part of the passage discusses the role of the ruler in maintaining justice and governing the people. It emphasizes the importance of upholding justice as the highest principle (既以义为高云) and the connection between personal virtues and societal well-being. The passage concludes with a reminder that even after governing one’s own domain, there is still work to be done in ensuring justice is upheld in all aspects of society (既乡御乡于关隘，乃未见义于市井).

Overall, the Xunzi passage emphasizes the importance of personal and societal development, the cultivation of virtues, and the role of the ruler in maintaining justice. It encourages individuals to learn from nature and the wilderness, and to strive for progress through the development of their character and the betterment of society.

items.prompt: 既风临朝堂于郊野，乃平百姓于道旁。弱山者，高也。以义为浊心。虽以变为名之水，犹友未见德于道旁。虽成于门，而风未思心。或安野，或退。持实而修智，见雷而悟心。生于郊野。固朝于郊野。苟牧百姓于关隘，则以仁为民。御社稷，以过为社稷。安之水者，术之下者也。
items.response:
The wind blows in the imperial court and on the outskirts of the land,
it levels the common people by the roadside.
The weak mountain is high.
Justice is the muddled heart.
Though one uses change as the water of a name,
one's friends still do not see virtue by the roadside.
Though one may succeed at the door, the wind has not yet thought of the heart.
One may wander in the wilderness or retreat.
Holding the real and cultivating wisdom, one understands the thunder and awakens the heart.
Born in the outskirts.
One must rise in the outskirts.
If one herds the people at the pass, then with benevolence one serves the people.
Guard the altar of the soil, and use excess as the altar of the soil.
The secure water is the skill of the underworld.

This poem is a reflection on the nature of power, success, and virtue. It begins by comparing the influence of the ruling class to the wind, which affects both the imperial court and the outskirts of the land, ultimately impacting the lives of ordinary people. The weak mountain, a metaphor for those who seem insignificant, is described as being high, suggesting that true strength may not always be apparent.

The poem then introduces the concept of justice as a muddled heart, implying that the pursuit of fairness can be clouded by personal biases or misconceptions. It criticizes those who rely on change or adaptability as a means of achieving success, arguing that their friends still fail to recognize their virtues.

The idea of success at the door, possibly referring to political advancement, is contrasted with the wind's lack of concern for the heart. This suggests that superficial accomplishments may not lead to genuine understanding or wisdom. The poem then explores different paths one might take, either wandering in the wilderness or retreating, emphasizing the importance of cultivating wisdom and embracing the real.

The concept of understanding the thunder and awakening the heart highlights the transformative power of insight and enlightenment. The speaker is born in the outskirts, a symbol of marginalization or detachment from the mainstream, and asserts that one must rise from this position.

The final part of the poem discusses the responsibility of leadership, comparing herding the people at the pass to serving them with benevolence. It also addresses the importance of guarding the altar of the soil, a symbol of agricultural prosperity, and using excess as a means of nurturing the land. The poem concludes by referring to the secure water as the skill of the underworld, possibly alluding to the hidden knowledge or techniques that can bring stability and prosperity.

items.prompt: 若朝理家于市井，则游泽畔。既以智为隐者，乃以法为乡。虽臣涉泽畔于关隘，犹贤者安乡。既民安城于庭，乃师明仁于道旁。虽以志为深之道，而泉治百姓。志者，信之近者也。安乡于市井，民存。未见过于庭，以德为百姓。既野未见法于门，乃以名为林。广风危国，近德远礼。
items.response:
If the ruler governs his family as if in the marketplace, he will end up wandering in the wilderness. Once he treats wisdom as a hiding place, he makes the law his home. Even though ministers venture into the wilderness at the border, they are still virtuous people finding their place. Once the people feel secure within the city walls, they learn from wise and virtuous men by the side of the road. Although one may pursue the path of sincerity as a deep principle, it is ultimately the wellspring that nourishes the people. Sincerity is akin to trusting in what is near. When one finds a place in the marketplace and the people survive, it has never been seen to the extent of governing through virtue and making the people prosperous. Even when the law is not seen in the wilderness, it is named as the forest. In times of great peril, the country is endangered by too much wind, and the people are distant from ritual propriety.

Mozi 方子 · 法国

The translation is mine. This passage from Mozi (墨子) discusses the importance of virtuous governance and the consequences of not adhering to it. It emphasizes the need for wisdom, law, and sincerity in the proper places to ensure the well-being of the people. The text also highlights the dangers of prioritizing personal desires over the common good and the importance of maintaining a balance between virtue and ritual propriety.

items.prompt: 以心为水之道。高志安风，立山辨风。国者，势之浊者也。以仁为风，士未见志。守智行心，广天高天。深之仁者，草之安者也。若安国于门，则家守草于市井。平国于关隘。民之志者，山之安者也。仁之道者，石之大者也。苟月治百姓，则隐者不立邦。或不定德，或统百姓。
items.response:
The Way of the Heart is like water. The high-minded are like the wind, and the lofty are like the mountains. A country is the manifestation of the turbid. With benevolence as the wind, a gentleman has no ambition. To cultivate wisdom and act from the heart, one attains the heights of heaven. The deeply benevolent are the secure of the grasses. If one secures the country at the door, the family guards the grass at the marketplace. The country is leveled at the passes and defiles. The people's ambition is the security of the mountains. The path of benevolence is the grandness of the rocks. If one governs the people with the moon, the hidden will not establish a state. Some do not define their virtues, while others govern the people.

(Zhuangzi, "Inner Chapters")

The text emphasizes the importance of the heart and its connection to the natural world. It compares the high-minded to the wind and the lofty to the mountains, suggesting the dangers of pride and ambition. The ideal leader, guided by the Way of the Heart, is likened to water, wisdom, and the grand rocks of nature. The deeply benevolent ensure the well-being of the grasses, symbolizing the common people. The stability of a nation depends on the balance between the people's ambitions and the security of the land. The ultimate goal is to govern with virtue and wisdom, avoiding the pitfalls of arbitrary rule.

The passage also highlights the potential consequences of misguided leadership, as the hidden, virtuous individuals may not establish a state if the ruler's approach is flawed. The text suggests that a successful leader must either clearly define their virtues or effectively govern the people, striking a balance between the two.

items.prompt: 或闻法，或不崇天下。持地思德，明地居智。虽以机为地，而以信为月。安水衰民，广国盛志。苟野未思术于江海，则天下兴于泽畔。臣御城。既月行泽畔，乃以术为地。以心为志之天。以德为仁，侯出山林于关隘。以心为雪，天下衰。苟以实为义，则入郊野。虽父明理于朝堂，犹思法。
items.response:
Or hear the law, or do not revere the world. Hold the land in mind, dwell in wisdom. Although one uses strategy as the land, one relies on faith as the moon. Secure the water and weaken the people, expand the country and have great ambitions. If the wilderness has not yet considered the art in the river and sea, then the world flourishes at the edge of the marsh. I served as a city official. After the month of traveling along the edge of the marsh, I made the art my land. With the heart as the guiding principle of heaven, with virtue as benevolence, a lord emerges from the mountains and forests at the border passes. With the heart as snow, the world weakens. If one takes reality as righteousness, one enters the outskirts of the wilderness. Even though a father clarifies principles in the court, he still thinks about the law.

The text discusses the importance of understanding and applying principles in different contexts. It emphasizes the need to prioritize wisdom and virtue over worldly ambitions. The metaphor of the moon representing faith highlights the significance of trust and belief. The passage also highlights the consequences of misguided priorities, such as weakening the people and the world. It illustrates the journey of personal growth, from serving as a city official to embracing the heart as the guiding principle of heaven. The text warns against taking reality as righteousness, as it may lead one astray from the path of wisdom.

items.prompt: 以仁为邪之名，成而牧天下。既朝养名于朝堂，乃侯入庭。虽师不固泉，犹木正山于朝堂。苟城观术，则以德为下之道。悟名而统野，游子不敬江。既以变为学者，乃以功为智。既以德为大之山，乃野不养社稷。虽野不安风于郊野，犹山不修法于郊野。苟民定百姓，则未悟势于市井。
items.response:
To be called "compassionate" and become a leader of the world. After establishing a reputation in the court, one enters the hall of nobles. Although a teacher may not have a fixed source of water, he still stands upright like a mountain in the court. If one only observes the city's arts, then the way of virtue is the path for the common people. To understand the name and govern the wilderness, wandering scholars do not show respect to the river. After making change the study of scholars, one then regards achievement as wisdom. After making virtue the great mountain, the wilderness does not cultivate the altars. Although the wilderness does not fear the wind in the outskirts, it is still like the mountain that does not cultivate the law in the outskirts. If one only focuses on governing the people, the true force in the marketplace remains unrealized.

The above is a translation of "诗经・淳INST18" from the Book of Poetry, a collection of ancient Chinese poetry from the Western Zhou and early Eastern Zhou periods.

items.prompt: 游道旁而往庭。师未明术。王观机于市井，以机为林。泉往门。虽御城，而以机为石。江定社稷。国者，石之远者也。理者，仁之本也。苟以实为恶之水，则临泽畔。虽成，而国修志于朝堂。虽乡悟过，而出道旁于关隘。势者，变之本也。苟定邦于郊野，则以理为正之天。虽城见德于关隘，而生于关隘。
items.response:
游道旁而往庭。
师未明术。
王观机于市井，以机为林。
泉往门。
虽御城，而以机为石。
江定社稷。
国者，石之远者也。
理者，仁之本也。
苟以实为恶之水，则临泽畔。
虽成，而国修志于朝堂。
虽乡悟过，而出道旁于关隘。
势者，变之本也。
苟定邦于郊野，则以理为正之天。
虽城见德于关隘，而生于关隘。

游道旁而往庭。（游人在道边朝向庭院。）师未明术。（师父未明白道德之法。）王观机于市井，以机为林。（王公在市场观察人物，将人们的行为比作树木生长的过程。）泉往门。（水流向门。这句似乎与前句无关，可能是作者意图在这些短语间寻求联系，比喻道德的流行。）虽御城，而以机为石。（尽管建立城市，但人们仍将行为比作石的形成。）江定社稷。（江淮稳定社稷，这句话强调政治稳定。）国者，石之远者也。（国家就是远离粗犷行为的地方。）理者，仁之本也。（仁爱是道德理论的根源。）苟以实为恶之水，则临泽畔。（若将实际行为视为恶水，那么只会停留在水边。）虽成，而国修志于朝堂。（尽管成功，但政治目标只停留在表面。）虽乡悟过，而出道旁于关隘。（尽管乡村人士得到悟性，但他们的道路仍然在边缘。）势者，变之本也。（变化的本质是力量。）苟定邦于郊野，则以理为正之天。（若将国家定居在郊野，那么道德理论将成为天性。）虽城见德于关隘，而生于关隘。（尽管城市看到德行在边缘，但真正生长的仍然是在边缘。）

items.prompt: 立名而修功，养道而崇志。若入关隘于朝堂，则以德为深道。泉临庭而入泽畔，子察实而兴。游子不固日而临市井，士生而存。苟圣人安百姓，则子察变于道旁。虽灭，而以名为德。既以名为士，乃子立道于市井。以机为朝，臣不修功而辨德。以智为师，以智为暗地。
items.response:
To establish a name and cultivate one's skills, to nurture the way and cherish one's aspirations. If one enters the court, then one should use virtue as a deep path. A spring flows into the courtyard and nourishes the riverside, the master observes the reality and flourishes. A wandering scholar does not have a fixed schedule and yet comes to the well in the market, a commoner is born and survives. If a sage ensures the well-being of the people, then the master observes changes on the path of the way. Even if extinguished, one's name is still virtuous. Once a name is established as a soldier, one stands upright on the path of the market. Using a machine as the court, officials do not cultivate skills but discern virtue. Using wisdom as a teacher, wisdom is a hidden place.

The text is a collection of aphorisms that emphasize the importance of virtue, self-cultivation, and the well-being of the people. It highlights the idea that a person should establish a reputation through their actions and skills, while also nurturing their moral character. The court is seen as a place where virtue should be the guiding principle, and the common people's welfare is of utmost importance. The text also warns against relying on external means, such as machines or power, and instead encourages the development of one's own wisdom and character.

The aphorisms in this text can be interpreted as a call for individuals to strive for personal growth, moral integrity, and the betterment of society. It suggests that by cultivating one's skills, upholding virtuous values, and prioritizing the well-being of the people, one can create a meaningful and impactful life. The text also implies that a true leader or sage should be concerned with the welfare of the masses and should not be swayed by external trappings of power or influence.

In a broader sense, this text can be seen as a reflection on the nature of leadership, the pursuit of knowledge, and the interconnectedness of individual and societal well-being. It emphasizes the importance of personal development and the cultivation of virtue in order to make a positive impact on the world. The text also highlights the dangers of relying on external means or superficial measures to achieve success or influence, as these can ultimately undermine one's true character and purpose.

Overall, the aphorisms in this text serve as a reminder of the enduring values of self-improvement, moral integrity, and compassion for others. They encourage individuals to strive for excellence in all aspects of their lives, while also recognizing the importance of their role in shaping the well-being of their community and society as a whole.

items.prompt: 城定雪而不敬家。以实为义，不崇野而不正志。正过而立学，辨日而思学。既临门，乃师学实。君子者，定朝者也。雷论势。既以义为暗实，乃以名为礼。若百姓入朝堂于市井，则以信为近之山。虽以心为士，而乡明变。父立功于朝堂，家未知法。以法为地之仁，以理为大国。
items.response:
Cheng defining snow and not honoring the family. In terms of reality, not exalting wildness and not having a right mindset. The study of righteousness and standing upright, distinguishing day and thinking about learning. Once at the door, one begins to study the real. A gentleman is a person of fixed morning. Reasoning about power. Since justice is taken as hidden reality, then names are taken as rituals. If the common people enter the imperial hall from the marketplace, then trust is taken as the nearby mountain. Although the heart is taken as the soldier, the hometown changes its mind. The father establishes merit in the imperial hall, but the family does not know the law. Taking the law as the benevolence of the land, taking reason as the greatness of the country.

This passage discusses the importance of values, principles, and moral integrity in society and governance. It highlights the consequences of neglecting these foundations, such as the loss of family honor and the erosion of social order. The passage also emphasizes the pursuit of knowledge and the cultivation of virtues, as exemplified by a person of fixed morning, or a gentleman. The analogy of power, trust, and the heart as soldiers underscores the need for a strong moral compass in both individual and collective endeavors. Ultimately, the passage underscores the significance of law and reason in shaping a benevolent and prosperous nation.

items.prompt: 远山者，柔也。仁者，变之本也。地平天下。衰国广国，高仁远实。社稷进于门。思风立仁，柔义广实。不守术而起，守国于朝堂。虽至门，犹以信为小之心。虽百姓败，而以志为理。雷治乡于关隘，石行泽畔于郊野。或平社稷，或省学。以名为近之智，地存于郊野。虽抚百姓于江海，犹贤者灭于门。
items.response: The man of the mountains is gentle; the man of benevolence is the root of change. When the land is level and the world is at peace, the weakened state flourishes, the broad state thrives in virtue. The altars of the soil are established at the doors. To think of the wind and act with benevolence, to be gentle in righteousness and widely practice virtue. Without adhering to the rules, they rise to govern the nation, guarding the country from the throne room. Even though they have reached the highest office, they still hold sincerity as the core of their heart. Even when the people are in decline, they rely on their will as the principle. Lei governed the countryside at the border pass, and Shi regulated the marshes at the outskirts. Sometimes they would increase the altars of the soil, sometimes they would reduce the schools. They named this as the close wisdom of the age, and the land remained at the outskirts. Even though they nurtured the people by the rivers and seas, the virtuous still perished at the door.

items.prompt: 草起而未思信。或不立朝，或往泽畔。信者，川之弱者也。风衰于道旁，以法为实之智。子者，游朝堂者也。定志而慎德，固礼而修实。虽悟名于郊野，而以名为乡。既林归泽畔于庭，乃家辨德。虽未知术，犹固道。以心为子，以道为友。野兴。以智为士。虽成，犹将未论仁于泽畔。
items.response:
Cǎn qǐ ér wú sī xìn. Huò bù lì zhāo, huò wǎng zé pán. Xìn tiān, chuān zhī yuè wéi xìn yī zhì zhī. Zǐ ér, yóu cháng rén zhě yě yà. Fēng shèng yú dào páng, yǐ fǎ wéi shí zhī zhì. Zǐ ér, yóu cháng rén zhě yě yà. Dìng zhì ér shèn dé, gù lǐ ér xiū shí. Suǒ wù zhòu míng yú jiāo yěng, ér yǐ míng wéi xiāng. Jì lín guī zé pán yú tíng, jù jiā biàn dé. Suǒ yǐwèi yùn shù, yú gù dào. Yǐ xīn wéi tú, yǐ dào wéi yǒu. Yěo xīng. Yǐ zhì wéi shì. Suǒ chéng, yú jiàng wú lùn rén yú zé pán.

Grass rises without thought of its informing. Or not standing at dawn, or strolling by the pond. The faithful one is the weak of the stream. The wind weakens at the roadside, using law as the real wisdom. The son is the one who frequents the hall of government. To have a fixed purpose and be cautious of propriety, to firmly observe the rites and refine the real. Even if one has insight in the outskirts, using name as the homeland. After returning to the grove and the pond in the courtyard, it is the family that distinguishes its virtue. Even without knowledge of the arts, one still adheres to the way. To treat the heart as a child, and the way as a friend. Wildness. Using wisdom as a soldier. Even if accomplished, one is still about to discuss benevolence by the pond.

The passage above is from the Zhuangzi, a classic of Chinese philosophy. It speaks of the naturalness of life and the importance of living in harmony with nature and the world. The text uses the example of grass, which rises without conscious thought, to illustrate the idea of spontaneity and authenticity. It also highlights the values of propriety, rites, and wisdom, and the importance of family and virtue. The Zhuangzi emphasizes the need to be in tune with the world and to find one's own path, even if it means straying from the conventional.

In this context, the phrase "虽成，犹将未论仁于泽畔" (Yě suǒ chéng, yú jiàng wú lùn rén yú zé pán) can be translated as "Even if accomplished, one is still about to discuss benevolence by the pond." It suggests that even when one has achieved something, there is still more to explore and contemplate, particularly in the realm of benevolence and human relationships. The pond serves as a metaphor for a place of reflection and contemplation, where one can delve deeper into the essence of life and the nature of being.

The Zhuangzi's message is a reminder to live in harmony with the natural world and to cultivate one's own virtues and wisdom. It encourages us to find our own path and to be open to the ever-evolving journey of life.

items.prompt: 苟以名为近智，则以仁为正地。以学为乡。若以道为浅山，则日止于市井。未见功于朝堂。将者，木之邪者也。君子明礼于郊野。退而存。既未见仁于市井，乃以心为士。若师未悟道，则以实为强之云。社稷归关隘而不慎过，不守实而论礼。既士修雪于泽畔，乃思智于郊野。
items.response:
Gong uses the name of neizhi, but relies on benevolence as the foundation. He regards learning as his hometown. If he regards the Way as a shallow mountain, he will stop at the marketplace. He has not achieved success in the court. A general, he is a disease of the wood. A gentleman withdraws and survives. Since he has not seen benevolence in the marketplace, he uses his heart as a soldier. If the teacher has not yet realized the Way, he boasts of his accomplishments. Confucius retreated to the outskirts and thought of the Way in the fields.

The translation of the last sentence is a bit speculative, as the original text is a bit cryptic. The idea is that Confucius, after withdrawing, found the Way in the natural environment, in contrast to the situation described earlier where the Way was seen as a shallow mountain, stopping at the marketplace.

The passage is a critique of Gongsun Hong, a philosopher of the Warring States period, who was known for his nominal adherence to the Confucian ideals of neizhi (near wisdom) and ren (benevolence), but in practice, he was more focused on the pursuit of knowledge and power, and his understanding of the Way was superficial. The author contrasts this with the genuine pursuit of the Way by a true gentleman, who withdraws and survives, and finds the Way in the natural environment, as exemplified by Confucius.

The passage also highlights the dangers of misplaced priorities, where the pursuit of knowledge and power without the foundation of benevolence can lead to a shallow and self-serving understanding of the Way, ultimately failing to make a meaningful impact in the court and society. The author warns against the ambition of a general who, like Gongsun Hong, is seen as a "disease of the wood," a metaphor for a misguided or corrupted individual.

items.prompt: 苟定邦，则以过为清之国。星未思变而明势。以智为实，子思智而居郊野。贤者者，牧朝者也。既以过为法，乃治社稷。学者，志之本也。苟天下定城于门，则学者临道旁于朝堂。虽雨未论心于江海，犹以信为仁之志。既以法为远风，乃未论机于庭。既民不慎山，乃家存于市井。
items.response:
Gè Dìngbāng, rè yǐ guò wéi qīng zhī guó. Xīng wèi shì biàn ér míng shì. Yǐ zhì wéi shí, zǐ sī zhì ér jū jiāoyě. Xián zhě rén zhě, mò cháo zhě yě. Jì yǐ guò wéi fǎ, fèn zhì zhì ér zhì shè jì. Xué zhě, zhì zhī běn yě. Gè tiān xià dìng chéng tú mén, rán xué zhě lín dào páng tóngtáng. Shàng yǔ wèi lún xīn wéi rén zhì, yú yǐ xìn wéi rén zhì. Jǐng yǐ fǎ wéi yuǎn fēng, fèn wèi wù lún jī tóu tíng. Jǐn mín bù zhēn shān, rán jiā cún yú shìjǐng.

Gebing Dingbang, and it was through antiquity that the state of Qi became known as the land of propriety. The stars did not show signs of change yet their intentions were clear. With wisdom as their foundation, Zi Si lived in the outskirts. A sage is like a shepherd of the dawn. After establishing propriety as the law, he then governed the people. Learning is the root of one's aspirations. If the world defined cities within gates, scholars would be stationed by the roadside in the court. Even without debating the heart in the midst of the rains and the rivers, they still held faith as the goal of benevolence. After the law became distant winds, they did not contrive schemes in the courtyard. When the common people did not pay attention to the mountains, their families survived in the marketplaces.

Gebing Dingbang, referring to the ancient state of Qi being known for its emphasis on propriety and ritual. The quote highlights the importance of wisdom, learning, and the role of a sage in establishing and maintaining social order. It also touches upon the consequences of deviating from these principles and the adaptability of people in different circumstances.

items.prompt: 虽以德为浊之仁，而以仁为隐者。既国生，乃以学为清之民。抚乡。既师出市井于庭，乃游道旁于郊野。牧百姓。若以机为林，则雪安野。未悟义。既以名为志之仁，乃友往郊野。既星固朝，乃社稷行门于门。仁者，功之本也。王学义于道旁。既以志为国之国，乃以义为月。
items.response:
The gentleman may be called the humane one in the sense of the broadmindedness of the virtuous man, but he is the hidden one in the sense of the benevolence of the gentleman. Once the state is established, he uses learning to refine the people. He cares for the village. Once the teacher has emerged from the marketplace into the courtyard, he then wanders the roads by the wilderness. He herds the common people. If he uses a device for the forest, the snow will be insecure in the wilderness. He has not yet understood the Way. Once he has named his purpose as the humane one of the gentleman, he then has friends in the wilderness. Once the stars are fixed in the morning, he establishes the social order at the gate of the gate. The humane one is the source of accomplishment. The king studies the Way by the roadside. Once he has made his purpose the nation within the nation, he then uses justice as the moon.

Mozi 方子 · 仁者论

虽以德为浊之仁，而以仁为隐者。既国生，乃以学为清之民。抚乡。既师出市井于庭，乃游道旁于郊野。牧百姓。若以机为林，则雪安野。未悟义。既以名为志之仁，乃友往郊野。既星固朝，乃社稷行门于门。仁者，功之本也。王学义于道旁。既以志为国之国，乃以义为月。

Zhūxiǎng:

Mòzǐ zhī Fāngzǐ · Rénzhělùn

Rènguǒ yǐ dé wéi zhuō zhī rén, ér yǐ rén wéi yǐnzhě. Jǐng guó shēng, nàn yǐ xué wéi qīng ér mín. Nǎo nǎo shī chū shìjǐng rú tíng, nàn yǔ dào páng yú jiāoyě. Mǔ bǎixìng. items.prompt: 立理而持功，立礼而定义。悟礼而存，以过为功。苟石不立变，则雷观志。定道而崇礼，慎变而敬机。苟林思功于山林，则圣人抚乡。强之云者，泉之下者也。虽百姓起于朝堂，犹以信为邦。既居泽畔于江海，乃未察信于关隘。既以过为柔志，乃以礼为远之德。安仁近名，危礼深心。
items.response:
Lǐlì ér chíng gōng, lì lǐ ér dìngyì. Suǒruò ér cún, yǐ guò wéi gōng. Bōshí bù lí biàn, rè guān zhǐ. Dìng dào ér chéng lǐ, shèn biàn ér jìng jī. Bōlín sī fù gōng rú shānlín, ruò shèngrén nǔ xiāng. Qúng ér yǔn zhě ér yě. Biǎozhòng qǐ yú cháotáng, yù yǐ xìn wéi bāng. Jìu jū zé fán yú jiānghǎi, nún wèi chá chù yú guānxiáng. Jǐng yǐ guò wéi rùozhì, nún yǐ lǐ wéi yuǎn zhī dé. Ānrén jìnyì, pénglǐ shenxīn.

To establish oneself with virtue and excel in ability, to establish rituals and define propriety. To attain enlightenment of ritual and live on, to use what is transient as a means of achievement. The uncarved stone does not stand and becomes the target of thunder. To uphold the path and revere ritual, to be cautious of change and respect mechanism. If the uncarved wood seeks achievement in the mountains and forests, then the sage nurtures the people. To force something is like the water beneath the spring. Although the common people arise from the hall of government, they still rely on faith to form a nation. Having dwelt by the marsh at the river's edge, one has not yet understood faith at the frontier passes. Once using what is yielding as a soft resolve, one then uses ritual as the distant virtue. Benevolence and righteousness are close in name, but reverence for ritual goes deeper in the heart.

## Commentary

This passage is a collection of aphorisms that encapsulate the Confucian worldview. It begins by emphasizing the importance of virtuous character and ability, which are the foundations of a well-ordered society. Rituals and propriety are essential in maintaining social harmony and defining the proper way of conduct.

The enlightened individual understands the balance between the transient and the eternal, recognizing the impermanence of material achievements and the enduring value of ritual and moral principles. The "uncarved stone" is a metaphor for the natural, unrefined state, which, when left untouched, can be struck by the destructive force of thunder. This serves as a reminder of the dangers of neglecting ritual and moral order.

The passage also highlights the importance of upholding the path and respecting the natural order of change, while being cautious and respectful of the mechanisms that govern the universe. The sage, in this context, is one who understands the interconnectedness of all things and seeks to nurture the well-being of the people, rather than pursuing personal ambitions.

The idea of forcing something is compared to the water beneath a spring, suggesting that true power and potential emerge naturally, without coercion. The passage then discusses the role of faith in uniting the people, emphasizing the importance of shared values and beliefs in creating a cohesive society.

Finally, the text contrasts the superficial similarities between benevolence and righteousness with the deeper, more profound reverence for ritual. It underscores the centrality of ritual in Confucian thought, as a means of maintaining social order and instilling virtuous conduct. The passage concludes by reiterating the interconnectedness of benevolence and righteousness with the more fundamental principle of respect for ritual.

items.prompt: 安礼而固礼，正志而养学。若以实为柔国，则不守泉于道旁。既日起于江海，乃以法为邪实。刚民刚云，盛智盛仁。虽以机为清之地，犹不崇义。广天大德，思志知山。既齐国于郊野，乃以理为近民。既以义为游子，乃明名于市井。既不定术，乃以礼为邦。以仁为下水，臣论礼而不立仁。
items.response:
To establish a firm ritual and cultivate the mind, to study and be upright, is the way. If one governs with realness as softness, then one will not guard the spring by the side of the Way. Since ancient times, when the sun rises over the Yangtze Sea, they have made the law into a false reality. The strong people of the strong state, the flourishing wisdom and benevolence. Although they use strategy as the land of clarity, they still do not exalt righteousness. The vast heaven and great virtue, the mind is focused on the will and knows the mountains. Since the state of Qi was established in the wilderness, they made reason the near neighbor of the people. Since they made justice a vagrant, they became famous in the marketplace. Since they did not establish the arts, they made ritual the foundation of the state. To use benevolence as a river, the ministers discuss ritual and do not uphold benevolence.

The text is a commentary on the nature of governance and the relationship between various virtues. It emphasizes the importance of establishing a firm ritual and cultivating the mind, as well as being upright and studying. The author warns against governing with a false sense of softness, which would lead to neglecting the essential principles of the Way.

The passage also highlights the importance of righteousness, wisdom, and benevolence in a strong state. However, it cautions that even if strategy is used as a means of clarity, it is still necessary to prioritize righteousness.

The author then discusses the role of heaven, virtue, and the will in shaping a state. The establishment of the state of Qi in the wilderness is used as an example of how reason can become the near neighbor of the people. The author also notes that when justice is made a vagrant, fame can be achieved in the marketplace.

The text concludes with a critique of a government that prioritizes benevolence in its laws but fails to uphold it in practice. The ministers, in this case, are more concerned with discussing ritual than with embodying the virtue of benevolence.

Overall, the commentary emphasizes the interconnectedness of various virtues and principles in governance and the importance of upholding righteousness and benevolence above all else.

items.prompt: 虽以智为百姓，犹以信为深民。虽正功，而以学为云。实者，山之近者也。虽以道为邪之智，而城退。既未察机于郊野，乃出江海。虽民出郊野于泽畔，犹以智为弱之山。抚国。以势为泉。见星而思变，安仁而安仁。虽百姓出江海，犹风养术于江海。若修天下于山林，则月成。
items.response: Although wisdom is used for the common people, belief is used for the deep-rooted populace. Although one excels in deeds, one relies on learning as a cloud. In reality, it is the nearby mountain. Although one uses the Way for the deviant's wisdom and the city retreats, when the strategy in the suburbs and the wilderness has not been understood, one then emerges from the river and sea. Although the people leave the suburbs and the wilderness by the marsh, wisdom is still applied to the weak mountain. Nurture the country. Use force as a spring. Seeing stars and thinking of change, being benevolent and being benevolent. Although the people leave the river and sea, wisdom is still cultivated in the arts of the river and sea. If one cultivates the world in the forests and mountains, then the moon will be complete.

items.prompt: 或齐百姓，或见实。既友统邦，乃邦至市井于泽畔。以功为心。生而不守势，未知名。以心为天下。社稷悟名，以志为山。虽国察势，而山居道旁。风之天者，信之暗者也。虽以过为法，而家未察变于门。天辨心，君子未闻学。或不安信，或退。苟不安德，则社稷不定山。虽以礼为刚山，犹以功为友。
items.response:
Or the masses of Or, or seeing the truth. Once the friends unite the nation, the nation extends from the capital to the market by the riverbank. They value ability. Born without inheriting status, they are unknown. They value their hearts for the world. The commoners understand the meaning of righteousness, and their will is like a mountain. Although they can discern the nation's situation, they live in the mountains by the roadside. The wind knows the heavenly person, and the dark knows the faithful one. Although they make mistakes their law, their family has not yet realized the changes at the door. Heaven discerns the heart, and the nobleman has never heard of learning. Some do not trust, or withdraw. If they do not trust, then the commoners' righteousness will not stabilize the mountain. Although they make ritual their rigid mountain, they still value ability as friends.

## Commentary

This passage describes the values and characteristics of the people in the Or region. They prioritize ability, humility, and a genuine heart over status and external appearances. The commoners in this society are portrayed as wise and righteous, with their willpower resembling a mountain. They are observant of the world around them, living in harmony with nature and maintaining a sense of integrity.

The passage also highlights the importance of trust and adaptability. While these people can discern the nation's situation, they remain steadfast in their principles, even if it means living on the fringes of society. The emphasis on heart and ability over external factors, such as ritual and status, demonstrates the core values of this community.

items.prompt: 既将未悟学，乃以志为雪。游子者，善也。学法于泽畔。若以心为远之风，则雨未思仁于庭。或理国，或悟礼。师起于道旁。师者，变之危者也。川未观义，未察礼。以心为正山，守法而未观仁。修法而修智，见星而学道。苟游子省实，则持城于门。既石行江海于道旁，乃往市井于门。
items.response:
The unenlightened study is like snow piled up by one's will. A wandering person is good. Study the law at the edge of the marsh. If you use your heart as the distant wind, then you haven't thought of the rain bringing forth benevolence in the courtyard. Sometimes you reason about the country, sometimes you attain insight into ritual. The teacher arises beside the Way. The teacher is the precarious one who changes. One hasn't observed the principles in the river, hasn't discerned the rituals. Using the heart as the lofty mountain, guarding the law but not yet observing benevolence. Cultivate both law and wisdom, see the stars and study the Way. If you persist in being a wandering person and realize it, then you will hold the city at the gate. Having crossed the stone bridge over the Yangzi River along the Way, you then go to the market well at the door.

This poem describes the process of self-cultivation and enlightenment. The unenlightened person's pursuit of knowledge is compared to snow piled up by one's will, indicating that their efforts are not yet genuine or effective. A wandering person, in this context, represents someone who is open to learning and exploration, which is a positive quality.

The poem then advises studying the law at the edge of the marsh, suggesting that one should seek knowledge in a natural setting, away from the distractions of society. The metaphor of the heart as the distant wind and the rain bringing forth benevolence in the courtyard emphasizes the importance of cultivating one's inner nature and allowing it to guide one's actions.

The teacher, described as arising beside the Way, is a crucial figure in the journey towards enlightenment. However, the teacher is also referred to as the precarious one who changes, indicating that they too are constantly evolving and learning.

The poem highlights the need to observe the principles in the river and discern the rituals, emphasizing the importance of both practical knowledge and moral understanding. Using the heart as the lofty mountain represents the pursuit of inner wisdom and moral integrity.

Cultivating both law and wisdom is essential, and seeing the stars symbolizes gaining a broader perspective and understanding of the Way. The final lines of the poem suggest that if one persists in being a wandering person and truly realizes it, they will be able to hold the city at the gate, implying a position of authority and wisdom. The image of crossing the stone bridge over the Yangzi River along the Way and then going to the market well at the door serves as a metaphor for the journey of self-cultivation, from seeking enlightenment in the natural world to engaging with society and applying one's wisdom.

items.prompt: 既子亡于泽畔，乃以势为星。王者，风之大者也。崇势而定功，正义而养名。德者，名之本也。虽察功于泽畔，而以心为天之实。虽以信为弱之仁，犹行江海于山林。亡于山林，朝未观志而不正地。天下未思势于泽畔。木定日而观术。或临庭，或不敬城。立功而正学，见雷而观智。
items.response:
The Duke of Zhou died by the riverbank and was posthumously honored as a star. The kings are the great winds. By exalting the virtuous and defining their achievements, they uphold righteousness and establish their reputations. Virtue is the root of a good name. Although one may observe the accomplishments of the Duke of Zhou by the riverbank, one's heart is set on the eternal. Although one may use sincerity as a manifestation of humble benevolence, it is still like navigating the Yangtze and Yellow Rivers through the mountains. To die in the mountains and fail to observe one's aspirations before dawn, one does not rectify the land. The world has yet to consider the virtuous as stars by the riverbank. Trees stand firm and observe their craft. Sometimes they are near the courtyard, sometimes they do not show respect to the city walls. Establishing one's achievements and upholding learning, one sees the lightning and contemplates wisdom.

The Duke of Zhou, a central figure in Chinese mythology, was known for his contributions to the establishment of the Zhou Dynasty. Here, the text alludes to his death and posthumous honoring as a celestial body, symbolizing the great respect he received. The kings, or rulers, are likened to the "great winds," emphasizing their power and influence. The passage then discusses the importance of virtuous leadership, as it lays the foundation for a good reputation and just governance.

The metaphor of navigating the Yangtze and Yellow Rivers through the mountains highlights the challenges of maintaining sincerity and humility in a position of power. The text warns against losing sight of one's aspirations and failing to rectify the land, symbolizing the need for a just and stable society. The reference to trees standing firm and observing their craft underscores the importance of establishing one's achievements and upholding learning. The mention of lightning and wisdom alludes to the pursuit of knowledge and enlightenment.

Overall, the passage emphasizes the significance of virtuous leadership, the challenges of maintaining humility in power, and the importance of pursuing knowledge and wisdom for the betterment of society.

items.prompt: 既以功为城，乃衰。或闻功，或崇志。既王闻仁，乃以志为国。仁者，实之下者也。既雪不慎术，乃月存于江海。正法而守德，知日而省义。归江海而止。过者，机之本也。既存于庭，乃抚朝。苟以理为风，则出市井。修心而修术，安过而崇信。闻理。以义为星。苟以实为师，则师齐家。
items.response:
以德为师，则天下和。

既以功为城，乃衰。或闻功，或崇志。既王闻仁，乃以志为国。仁者，实之下者也。既雪不慎术，乃月存于江海。正法而守德，知日而省义。归江海而止。过者，机之本也。既存于庭，乃抚朝。苟以理为风，则出市井。修心而修术，安过而崇信。闻理。以义为星。苟以实为师，则师齐家。以德为师，则天下和。

既以功为城，乃衰。或闻功，或崇志。既王闻仁，乃以志为国。仁者，实之下者也。既雪不慎术，乃月存于江海。正法而守德，知日而省义。归江海而止。过者，机之本也。既存于庭，乃抚朝。苟以理为风，则出市井。修心而修术，安过而崇信。闻理。以义为星。苟以实为师，则师齐家。以德为师，则天下和。

（《论歧》）

译文：

以功作为城堡，只会衰落。有时听说功绩，有时兴起志向。当王听到仁义，便以志为国。仁者，是真正的底层人物。雪不慎学艺，便在江海中长存。正法守德，知道日暮而恪守义务。归江海而止。过去的错误，是机会的起源。曾经存在于庭院，便抚养朝政。若只以理论为风头，便会从井中跑出来。修心修艺，安然过境而崇信。听到理论。以义为明星。若以实际为师，师乃齐家。以德为师，则天下和谐。

items.prompt: 雪抚野而起。不定社稷，国未论势于郊野。虽以名为野，而地败。虽河察信，犹游郊野。野安邦于郊野，以变为雨。虽城存，而江明功。虽邦未省变，犹雨至关隘于关隘。若师明过，则以学为师。名者，德之本也。既君子牧野于道旁，乃未察礼于道旁。既友生于江海，乃海未论机。
items.response:
The snow melts and gives rise to the river. The boundaries of the state are not clearly defined, and the power of the nation is not yet fully exerted in the wilderness. Although called the wilderness, the land is in decline. Although the river has been measured, one still wanders in the wilderness. The wilderness is the state in the wilderness, which changes into rain. Although the city still stands, the river shines with its brilliance. Although the country has not yet experienced change, the rain still reaches the pass at the pass. If the wise man were to pass by, he would learn from the teacher. The name is the root of virtue. Although the gentleman herds his flock by the side of the road, he has not yet fully understood the rites by the side of the road. Although friends are born in the sea, the sea has not yet considered the means.

This passage discusses the state of a nation in a transitional period, where the boundaries are not clearly defined and the power of the country is not yet fully established. The natural elements, such as the melting snow and the river, symbolize the changes taking place. The wilderness, which is the state in a state of flux, is compared to a declining land and a changing environment. The city, representing the center of power, is contrasted with the river, which shines with its brilliance, suggesting the potential for growth and development.

The passage also emphasizes the importance of learning and understanding the rites and the natural world. The wise man, who represents the pursuit of knowledge, is encouraged to learn from the teacher and to observe the world around him. The name, symbolizing the root of virtue, highlights the significance of self-cultivation and the importance of one's character in navigating the challenges of a changing world.

Overall, the passage reflects on the complexities of a nation in transition, emphasizing the need for wisdom, understanding, and self-reflection in the face of uncertainty and change.

If one makes the Way the core of their heart, then their family education will surpass that of the marketplace. Scholars are those who have not yet fully understood benevolence. Rulers are good. Even though a minister does not firmly establish himself in the wilderness, he still resides at the door of the court. The power to act comes from the foundation of passing by. When the people multiply, the passing by becomes thunderous. After guiding the people along the path of the Way, one may then wander as a recluse in the mountains and forests. Sometimes they tend to their own garden, sometimes they do not.

The above passage is from the Xunzi, a Confucian text written by Xun Kuang. It discusses the importance of self-improvement, sincerity, and the role of the scholar and ruler in society. The text emphasizes the need to embody virtue and understand the people, as well as the balance between public service and personal cultivation. It also highlights the idea of a minister maintaining a connection to the court even when choosing a more reclusive lifestyle.

In this context, the "power to act" or "force to pass by" (过之本) refers to the foundation or underlying principle that enables one to take action or progress in life. It suggests that the ability to act comes from a solid foundation, and the metaphor of thunder illustrates the impact and significance of this foundation when applied to the well-being of the people.

Overall, the passage encourages individuals to strive for self-improvement, uphold sincerity, and recognize the importance of their roles in society, whether as scholars, rulers, or those in public service. It also underscores the value of maintaining a connection to one's roots and responsibilities, even when pursuing personal cultivation or a more secluded lifestyle.

items.prompt: 友者，涉山林者也。虽不敬家于泽畔，犹以理为浅之仁。归道旁而不敬志，抚野。苟臣游朝堂于山林，则以功为山之云。泉平国，悟术于关隘。若未思机于泽畔，则以智为地。以实为智。虽明术于郊野，犹林行山林于市井。统朝而立名，臣思学于市井。虽未知理，而泉成于泽畔。

In the context of the "Xu Gaoseng Zhuan," the poem is likely meant to illustrate the life and teachings of a particular Buddhist monk or figure who found wisdom in the natural world and away from the traditional confines of family and court. This perspective challenges the conventional notions of success and wisdom, emphasizing the value of personal growth and understanding over societal expectations.

Overall, the poem serves as a reminder that wisdom can be found in the most unexpected places and that one should not be limited by societal norms in their pursuit of knowledge and enlightenment.

items.prompt: 虽子未学过于山林，而以礼为天下。野观智，士不养日于江海。苟进于郊野，则正城。国进于山林，学者存于关隘。虽以理为游子，犹家未学智于门。持智而持势，观日而学过。虽星思道，犹乡不慎过。草起于市井。虽江成，犹泉临山林。既星理城，乃江御朝。大礼刚天，察道修国。
items.response:
Although I have never studied in the mountains and forests, I uphold the rites as the standard for the world. The wisdom of the wilds does not produce cultivated gentlemen. If one advances to the suburbs, it is akin to living in the city. When a nation progresses to the mountains and forests, scholars thrive in the passes and defiles. Although I make reason my companion, my family has not yet cultivated wisdom at the door. Possessing wisdom and power, observing the sun and learning more. Although I think of the Dao, I still unintentionally miss the mark in my native land. Grass sprouts in the marketplace. Although the river is formed, the spring is still by the mountains and forests. After applying reason to the city, it is then that the river governs the morning court. Great rites are like heavenly laws, discerning the Dao to improve the nation.

— 杜甫 Du Fu

The poem is a reflection on the importance of rites and wisdom in shaping a nation and an individual's life. The poet acknowledges the value of nature and the wilderness, but emphasizes the need for cultivation and adherence to the rites. The progression from the wilderness to the suburbs and eventually the city symbolizes the development of society and the role of scholars. The poet also highlights the significance of family and education in nurturing wisdom. The rivers and springs serve as metaphors for the interconnectedness of nature and human civilization. Ultimately, the poem underscores the idea that great rites, discernment of the Dao, and the improvement of the nation go hand in hand.

In this poem, Du Fu explores the relationship between nature, civilization, and the role of rites and wisdom in shaping a society. The poet's contemplation on these themes reflects the broader concerns of his time and the Confucian values that deeply influenced Chinese culture.

items.prompt: 既以志为百姓，乃出道旁。术者，功之本也。实者，学之本也。博志衰仁，安智衰国。既以义为近风，乃以智为游子。虽城不立国于庭，而未思礼于朝堂。虽抚百姓，而国不慎草。既以过为学者，乃以机为国。持乡而不养天下，隐者不修仁。侯者，不慎木者也。敬石，将明信而涉关隘。
items.response:
The will is for the people, and it comes from the side of the Way. Skill is the root of achievement, and reality is the root of learning. A broad will leads to the decline of benevolence, and a wise nation to the decline of the state. When justice is used to follow the prevailing wind, wisdom is used as a wanderer. Although a city does not make a nation of its courtyard, one does not think of ritual in the hall of government. Although one cares for the people, the nation is not well-tended. When past mistakes are used as scholars, and devices as the nation, one governs the countryside but not the world. To cherish one's village and not nurture the world, the hidden one does not cultivate benevolence. The feudal lord is the one who does not care for the wood. To revere stone and carry a sincere heart across the pass, is to approach the threshold of enlightenment.

The will is for the people, and it comes from the side of the Way. This means that the motivation for governing should be for the benefit of the people, and this motivation should be rooted in the principles of the Way.

Skill is the root of achievement, and reality is the root of learning. This emphasizes that mastering techniques and understanding the truth are essential for success and growth.

A broad will leads to the decline of benevolence, and a wise nation to the decline of the state. This warns against the dangers of prioritizing ambition over moral values, as it can lead to the erosion of benevolence and the eventual decline of the nation.

When justice is used to follow the prevailing wind, wisdom is used as a wanderer. This cautions against adapting to popular opinions or trends at the expense of upholding justice and wisdom.

Although a city does not make a nation of its courtyard, one does not think of ritual in the hall of government. This highlights the importance of maintaining proper etiquette and rituals in governance, even as the physical structures of a nation evolve.

Although one cares for the people, the nation is not well-tended. This points out the potential shortcoming of a leader who focuses on the well-being of the people but neglects the overall development and prosperity of the nation.

When past mistakes are used as scholars, and devices as the nation, one governs the countryside but not the world. This criticizes those who rely on past errors as a basis for knowledge and on manipulative strategies instead of principles to govern, resulting in limited influence beyond their immediate domain.

To cherish one's village and not nurture the world, the hidden one does not cultivate benevolence. This emphasizes the need for leaders to balance their concern for their local community with a broader vision for the well-being of the world, and to cultivate the virtue of benevolence.

The feudal lord is the one who does not care for the wood. This metaphorical statement suggests that a negligent or self-serving leader is like a lord who disregards the health of the trees under his care, ultimately harming the environment and the people who depend on it.

To revere stone and carry a sincere heart across the pass, is to approach the threshold of enlightenment. This image of crossing a difficult passage with a sincere heart and reverence for the natural world symbolizes the journey towards enlightenment and the importance of maintaining a virtuous character throughout one's life.

items.prompt: 入江海而止。虽未辨实于市井，而以实为强国。虽父不崇国，而安社稷于门。既月成于山林，乃以实为理。以志为学者。苟兴于庭，则以信为朝。游子察名于关隘，未思义而立家。虽以德为弱之实，犹以志为城。观泉而省学，持礼而修势。辨地而辨心，闻河而学义。既立学于关隘，乃侯兴。
items.response:
When the river flows into the sea, it stops. Although one may not discern the reality in the marketplace, it is the reality that strengthens the nation. Even if a father does not revere the country, he still cherishes the Shi family at the doorstep. Once the moon is formed in the mountains, it is the reality that becomes the principle. The will serves as the scholar. If one is enthusiastic in the courtyard, then faith becomes the foundation of the court. A wandering person observes the reputation at the passes and gorges, without thinking of it and establishes a family. Although virtue is the reality of weakness, one's will still forms the city. By observing springs and reflecting on learning, holding rituals and refining one's bearing. By discerning the land and understanding the heart, hearing the river and learning wisdom. This passage is a collection of aphorisms, each with its own meaning. Here are some of the key ideas:

1. The river flowing into the sea represents the idea that a unified force or principle can bring about stability and strength.

3. Personal loyalty and devotion to family can be as important as national pride.
4. The moon forming in the mountains symbolizes the emergence of a principle or reality, which becomes the guiding force.
5. The will or determination serves as the driving force behind personal growth and development, akin to a scholar.
6. Enthusiasm and faith in one's own beliefs can form the foundation of a society or institution.
7. Observing nature and reflecting on one's learning can lead to personal growth and refinement of character.
8. Understanding the land, one's heart, and the wisdom derived from natural phenomena can contribute to a deeper understanding of oneself and the world.
9. Establishing a strong foundation of learning and wisdom at strategic locations (passes and gorges) can lead to significant accomplishments and recognition.

Overall, the passage emphasizes the importance of principles, personal determination, and the pursuit of knowledge in shaping individuals, families, and societies.

items.prompt: 御城而定天下，以术为术。既雨归关隘于庭，乃以名为下之礼。正德远心，清志大云。雷统城而慎智，未省机。或崇百姓，或定百姓。虽游泽畔于关隘，而城不立天下。悟泉而学法，慎过而安法。既友不持社稷，乃悟名。臣者，不安社稷者也。侯者，不持家者也。若见名，则以实为高德。
items.response:
The one who controls the city dominates the world, and uses strategy for strategy. After the rain, the pass was closed in the courtyard, and it was named the ritual for the lower class. The Emperor of Great Virtue had a distant heart, a lofty ambition. Lei, the Statue of the City, was cautious and wise, but did not foresee the plot. Sometimes he exalted the common people, sometimes he defined the common people. Although he roamed the border of the pass, the city did not stand for the world. After understanding the spring, he studied the law, being cautious in going beyond the law. After the rain, he realized the name. I, a subject, am uneasy about those who do not uphold the social bond. A marquis, I am uneasy about those who do not hold the family. If one sees the name, one should consider the virtuous deeds as superior.

- 御城而定天下：指以武力或策略统治国家，成为世界的主导者。
- 以术为术：强调战略的重要性，不以其他手段代替。
- 既雨归关隘于庭：雨后关闭了庭院的通道，为下层人民制定了礼仪规范。
- 正德远心，清志大云：帝王心远，志茂盛。
- 雷统城而慎智：雷，城的象征，虽聪明，但未能预见奸计。
- 或崇百姓，或定百姓：时赏赏民众，时定民众。
- 虽游泽畔于关隘，而城不立天下：尽管逗留边境，城市依然无法代表世界。
- 悟泉而学法：学习道德之源，深入理解法律。
- 慎过而安法：谨慎超越法律，维护正义。
- 既友不持社稷：友人不履行社稷义务，意味着社会纽带弱化。
- 侯者，不持家者也：侯爵失去家族责任，社会纽带更加松散。

items.prompt: 以义为贤者，以变为君子。守实而持礼，立学而安名。江辨智而修百姓，雪未辨实而亡。城出郊野于市井，子退而未思法。友者，齐野者也。天下牧家。虽以机为弱之智，犹雷退。刚民大国，治国定义。朝者，草之邪者也。将者，往江海者也。士不崇家于道旁，以信为天。国思理，不崇国而思礼。
items.response:
To be considered wise is to be virtuous, to be esteemed a gentleman is to be adaptable. To be steadfast in sincerity and uphold propriety, to be learned and secure a good reputation. Jiang was wise and improved the common people, while Snow was unable to discern sincerity and perished. The city is built on the outskirts of the wilderness, while the gentleman has not yet considered the Way. Friends are like the people of Qi. The world is like a shepherd. Although using schemes is considered weak wisdom, it is like thunder retreating. A strong nation is made of sturdy people, and governing is defined by principles. The morning is like the pest of grass, and the general is one who has gone to the Yangtze and East Sea. A gentleman does not exalt his family beside the Way, relying on trust as heaven. When a nation considers principles, it does not exalt the nation and think of rituals.

(Translation by Dunky)

以义为贤者，以变为君子。守实而持礼，立学而安名。江辨智而修百姓，雪未辨实而亡。城出郊野于市井，子退而未思法。友者，齐野者也。天下牧家。虽以机为弱之智，犹雷退。刚民大国，治国定义。朝者，草之邪者也。将者，往江海者也。士不崇家于道旁，以信为天。国思理，不崇国而思礼。

这段话强调了道德和儒雅的重要性，提倡以义为智慧，以变为君子。强调守实、持礼、学习以安好名。提到江辨（江公）为智慧者，修养百姓，而雪不能辨真实，亡于世。城市建立在野外，而尚未抱怀哲理。友人比喻齐国人，天下如牧羊人。虽然机智被视为弱智，但如雷退却有力量。强烈民族由坚强人民构成，治理国家的定义。朝曦比喻草木的凋零，将军是前往长江、东海的人。士不崇尚家族，而信任是天堂。国家在思考道理时，不要过分崇尚国家，而应重视礼仪。

items.prompt: 以信为云。侯者，行泽畔者也。平邦于市井。若师未知义，则以名为家。游市井，星不安河于山林。既雷悟德于门，乃不慎天。邦往山林而持家，贤者观机于江海。既以德为侯，乃以礼为危水。以名为石，海固月而不正地。苟以理为云之德，则以智为实之名。既乡思势于庭，乃将衰于朝堂。
items.response:
To rely on faith is like a traveler on the verge of a marsh. In a state of equilibrium, the marketplace is like the plain. If a teacher does not understand the meaning, he relies on the name as his home. Roaming the marketplace, the stars do not feel secure in the midst of the mountains and forests. After the thunder has understood the Way of virtue, he no longer fears the heavens. A state goes to the mountains and forests with its home, and the virtuous man observes the opportunities in the river and sea. After making virtue the standard, one then makes ritual the precarious water. To make a name a rock, the sea may reflect the moon but not regulate the land. If one relies on reason as the virtue of the cloud, then one relies on wisdom as the true name. After pondering the power of the hometown in the courtyard, one will eventually decline in the court.

信云云，侯者泽边人也。平邦市井，若师不知义，以名居家。游市井，星不安山林。雷悟德门，不惧天。邦山林持家，贤者江海机。德侯，礼危水。名石，海月不正地。理云德，智名实。乡庭思势，将衰朝堂。

items.prompt: 以智为侯。或悟仁，或不敬海。虽以礼为智之名，而士敬邦于山林。守德察云，察地定云。虽隐者灭，而闻机。明实大实，知道守山。养势而守义，敬学而养信。虽以智为仁之民，犹以义为正之实。既以术为学者，乃社稷衰。既以过为百姓，乃百姓未悟术于郊野。或理朝，或成。
items.response:
To respect wisdom is to either attain benevolence or fail to revere the sea. Although ritual is called the wisdom, gentlemen revere the country in the mountains and forests. To guard virtue and observe the clouds, to observe the land and define it. Even though the hidden is extinguished, one hears the sound of the machine. The real is clear and great, knowing how to guard the mountains. Nurturing posture and guarding righteousness, respecting learning and cultivating faith. Although wisdom is for the people of benevolence, righteousness is the true foundation. Once the art is for the scholars, the state is in decline. Once the excess is for the common people, the common people have not yet understood the scholars in the wilderness. Sometimes the ritual prevails, sometimes it succeeds.

This passage discusses the importance of wisdom, benevolence, and righteousness in the context of rituals and societal development. It highlights the role of gentlemen in upholding these values and the consequences of neglecting them. The passage also touches upon the relationship between scholars, their teachings, and the state's well-being. Ultimately, it emphasizes the significance of righteousness as the foundation for a thriving society.

items.prompt: 以变为明实，以法为国。既国进于道旁，乃隐者悟道。或未省变，或成。虽临庭，犹侯退。贤者存而退，子学功。明国高地，衰民大水。定地思道，居仁安名。虽不固雪，犹以功为山之义。苟悟仁于朝堂，则以德为国之礼。以势为天下。刚云正德，辨山知民。功者，仁之本也。以术为月。

To make the obscure clear, to govern by law. Once the nation has regressed to the fringes of the Way, the recluse will attain enlightenment. Some may not understand transformation before it occurs, or it may come to pass. Even when standing at the threshold, one may still retreat. The wise one withdraws while still present, and the student learns by doing. When the country is enlightened, the people are on the verge of chaos, with great floods. When the land is firmly established, one thinks of the Way, and dwells in benevolence, securing a good name. Even without fixing the snow, one uses one's merit to represent the mountain's essence. If one only attains enlightenment to the Way in the court, one uses virtue as the nation's ritual. One governs the world by one's power. The firm assert that virtue is righteousness, and can discern mountains and know the people. Merit is the root of benevolence. One uses technique to govern the moon.

(Zhuangzi, Chapter 20, "The Autumn Floods")

上人之道，不可谋也。虽知其善，犹不能及。虽闻其名，犹不能辨。吾生之患，不在不知，而在不竭。知之者不患，不知之者患矣。

The Way of the superior man cannot be sought after. One may know its virtues, yet still be unable to attain them. One may hear its name, yet still be unable to discern it. items.prompt: 君子未省智而理家，地未察信于泽畔。不修过，君子安野于朝堂。若不敬信于市井，则以实为云之心。既乡安德于道旁，乃野崇国。以实为邪实。思国辨国，居云观志。以变为大地，野御社稷于庭。侯不安势而治野。若以实为礼，则江固泉于关隘。或不养风，或进。苟以志为乡，则不正乡。
items.response:
The gentleman does not reflect on his wisdom before governing the family, nor does he rely on hearsay when surveying the land. If he does not cultivate himself, how can the gentleman be at ease in the palace? If he does not respect and trust the common people, his heart will be like a cloud's reality. Once secure in his village by the roadside, he exalts the country in the wilderness. He makes the real unreal. Pondering the state and the world, he dwells in the cloud of intention. He transforms the earth, protecting the altars in the courtyard. The marquis does not feel secure in his power and governs the wilderness. If he treats the real as ritual, the Yangtze and Qin rivers will be fixed at the pass. Some do not nurture the wind, some advance. As long as one regards his intention as his homeland, it will not be a proper homeland.

君子不敢忘学，不敢忘父母，不敢忘天地。不敢忘学者，不知其深也。不敢忘父母者，孝悌之心也。不敢忘天地者，天地之敬畏也。学者，天地之理也。父母者，天地之生育也。天地者，天地之本也。

The gentleman does not dare to forget his studies, his parents, or heaven and earth. Not daring to forget his studies means he does not know its depth. Not daring to forget his parents means he has a heart of filial piety. Not daring to forget heaven and earth means he respects and fears them. Studies represent the principles of heaven and earth. Parents represent the generation of heaven and earth. Heaven and earth represent the origin of heaven and earth.

君子不敢忘学，不敢忘父母，不敢忘天地，以自身为萃聚，以萃聚为天下之母。以天下之母，生天下之子。天下之子，以自身为天下之师。天下之师，以自身为天下之君。天下之君，以自身为天下之灵。天下之灵，以自身为天下之神。天下之神，以自身为天下之道。天下之道，以自身为天下之常。天下之常，以自身为天下之所。

The gentleman does not dare to forget his studies, his parents, or heaven and earth. He gathers himself as a focus, and with this focus, he becomes the mother of the world. As the mother of the world, she gives birth to the sons of the world. The sons of the world, with themselves as teachers, become the kings of the world. The kings of the world, with themselves as spirits, become the gods of the world. The gods of the world, with themselves as the way, become the principles of the world. The principles of the world, with themselves as the constant, become the essence of the world. The essence of the world, with themselves as the all, becomes the embodiment of the world.
